file_name: VirtuosoSurgicalInc_20191227_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_11933379_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_License Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 "LICENSED PRODUCT(S)" as used herein in either singular or plural shall mean any material, compositions, or other product, the manufacture, use or sale of which would constitute, but for the license granted to Company pursuant to this Agreement, an infringement of a claim of PATENT RIGHT(S) (infringement shall include, but is not limited to, direct, contributory, or inducement to infringe).
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 "LICENSED PRODUCT(S)" as used herein in either singular or plural shall mean any material, compositions, or other product, the manufacture, use or sale of which would constitute, but for the license granted to Company pursuant to this Agreement, an infringement of a claim of PATENT RIGHT(S) (infringement shall only include direct infringement).
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the definition of 'LICENSED PRODUCT(S)' by limiting the scope of infringement to only direct infringement, removing contributory or inducement to infringe. This creates a contradiction with Section 5.2 Right to Enforce: JHU shall have the first right to enforce the PATENT RIGHTS against any infringement or alleged infringement thereof in the LICENSED FIELD OF USE, this section originally applied for both direct, contributory, or inducement to infringe, which would cause confusion of its enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2 Right to Enforce: JHU shall have the first right to enforce the PATENT RIGHTS against any infringement or alleged infringement thereof in the LICENSED FIELD OF USE.
perturbation.justification: The change to Section 2.3 limits the definition of 'LICENSED PRODUCT(S)' to only direct infringement, removing contributory or inducement to infringe. This directly contradicts Section 5.2, which grants JHU the right to enforce PATENT RIGHTS against any infringement, which initially included direct, contributory, or inducement to infringe. By narrowing the definition of infringement in 2.3, the scope of enforcement rights in 5.2 becomes unclear and contradictory, creating an in-text contradiction. As per the definition provided, ambiguities occur when key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 License Granted: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement and to non-exclusive license agreements executed prior to the EFFECTIVE DATE, JHU hereby grants to the Company a non-exclusive, non-transferable license to make, have made, import, offer for sale and sell the LICENSED PRODUCT(S) and the LICENSED SERVICE(S) in the United States and worldwide under the PATENT RIGHT(S) in the LICENSED FIELD.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 License Granted: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement and to non-exclusive license agreements executed prior to the EFFECTIVE DATE, JHU hereby grants to the Company a non-exclusive, non-transferable license to make, have made, import, offer for sale and sell the LICENSED PRODUCT(S) and the LICENSED SERVICE(S) solely in the United States under the PATENT RIGHT(S) in the LICENSED FIELD.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the geographic scope of the license to 'solely in the United States', it now contradicts the original allowance for sales 'worldwide'. This introduces ambiguity because Section 6.1 specifies the term of the agreement continues in 'each country', but the license is now only for the US. The contradiction lies in whether the agreement and its termination clauses still apply to territories outside the US, despite the restriction on sales.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 Expiration: This term of this Agreement shall commence on the EFFECTIVE DATE and shall continue, in each country, until the date of expiration of the last to expire patent within PATENT RIGHT(S) in that country.
perturbation.justification: The original text granted a license to make and sell products worldwide, while the changed text restricts this license to 'solely in the United States.' This directly contradicts Section 6.1, which states that the agreement continues 'in each country' until the expiration of the last patent. The contradiction arises because the agreement outlines terms and termination clauses that would ostensibly apply to territories outside the US, even though sales are now restricted to the US only.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 License Fee: As consideration for JHU's grant of a License to Company under this Agreement, Company shall pay to JHU a license fee, cash and equity, as set forth in Exhibit A within thirty (30) days of the EFFECTIVE DATE. The license fee is nonrefundable and shall not be credited against royalties or other fees.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 License Fee: As consideration for JHU's grant of a License to Company under this Agreement, Company shall pay to JHU a license fee, cash and equity, as set forth in Exhibit A within thirty (30) days of the EFFECTIVE DATE. The license fee is refundable and shall be credited against royalties or other fees.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the license fee from 'nonrefundable and shall not be credited against royalties or other fees' to 'refundable and shall be credited against royalties or other fees'. This creates a direct contradiction in the terms of payment. Contradicting section: Section 4.2 Minimum Annual Royalties: Company shall pay to JHU minimum annual royalties as set forth in Exhibit A. This section originally was not considering any credit towards the minimun anual royalties to be paid to JHU, now with this change it can cause a contradiction about how the payments are going to be made.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Minimum Annual Royalties: Company shall pay to JHU minimum annual royalties as set forth in Exhibit A. These minimum annual royalties shall be due within thirty (30) days of each anniversary of the EFFECTIVE DATE beginning with the first anniversary. Running royalties accrued under Paragraph 4.3 and paid to JHU during the one year period preceding an anniversary of the EFFECTIVE DATE shall be credited against the minimum annual royalties due on that anniversary date.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the license fee is 'nonrefundable and shall not be credited against royalties or other fees.' The changed text makes the license fee 'refundable and shall be credited against royalties or other fees.' This directly contradicts the original terms of payment outlined in Section 4.1. Furthermore, crediting the license fee against royalties creates a conflict with Section 4.2, which specifies how running royalties are credited against minimum annual royalties. The changed text introduces a new, conflicting method of crediting fees, causing a strong in-text contradiction regarding payment obligations.
